text:
The purpose of this study was to investigate the effects of different solvent mixtures on the yields of phenolic compounds and anthocyanins, as well as their antioxidant activities, from black mulberry (Morus nigra), blackberry (Rubus ulmifolius) and strawberry (Fragaria x ananassa) fruits. In total, 19 different mixtures were prepared from water, methanol, ethanol, and acetone as single solvents, or as their mixtures in different acidic conditions. The antioxidant activities of the extracts obtained with different solvents were determined using FRAP, DPPH• scavenging, and ORAC assays, and the relationships between the extraction variables were clarified by principal component analysis (PCA).

Solvent polarity and composition have been investigated in relation to the recovery of bioactive compounds from various berries using liquid extraction methods. Results showed that all the fruits yielded the desired bioactive compounds in varying degrees when extracted with aqueous acetone or aqueous/alcoholic acid mixtures. A 70% aqueous acetone mixture was found to be the optimal extractant for the total phenolic content in all the fruits, while acidified 70% aqueous methanol yielded the highest amount of anthocyanins. The optimal solvent for maximum antioxidant capacity of the fruits varied with the species of fruit under investigation. For mulberries, the solvent was found to be 50% aqueous ethanol; while for blackberries and strawberries, the corresponding solvent mixtures were 70% acidified aqueous acetone and 50% aqueous acetone respectively. Total phenolic content was found to be significantly correlated with the antioxidant capacity of all the extracts employing FRAP and DPPH• assays. Furthermore, the use of multivariate analysis - principal components analysis (PCA) - revealed that the organic solvent-water mixtures were better suited to extract the desired bioactive compounds from the fruits under investigation when compared with the use of pure organic solvents or water alone.
